preamble: The deletion of the velar nasal /ŋ/ in word-initial position is a documented sound change across unrelated language families, driven by the cross-linguistic rarity and articulatory difficulty of word-initial /ŋ/ in many phonological systems. It is attested in ongoing change in Hong Kong Cantonese, where the onset /ŋ/ is merging with a zero onset, and as a completed change in dialects of Bininj Kunwok (Northern Australia).

examples.note: 我 'I/me'; word-initial /ŋ/ is deleted, merging with the zero-onset class. The change is widespread in younger speakers and is stigmatized by prescriptivists as 'lazy pronunciation' (懶音).
牛 'cow'; another high-frequency word where onset /ŋ/ is variably deleted.
Buffalo (loanword); word-initial /ŋ/ has been deleted in dialects of this non-Pama-Nyungan language of western Arnhem Land, Northern Territory, Australia.

sources: Lam, S.-Y. (2024). A phonological analysis of the onset velar nasal merger in Hong Kong Cantonese. Proceedings of the Linguistic Society of America, 9(1), 5727.
Marley, A. H. (2022). Sound change in Aboriginal Australia: word-initial engma deletion in Kunwok. Linguistics Vanguard, s5, 645-659.
